elements.sent-2411-001: The strong performance of large language models (LLMs) on natural language processing tasks raises extensive discussion on their application to code generation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent work suggests multiple sampling approaches to improve initial code generation accuracy or program repair approaches to refine the code.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, these methods suffer from LLMs' inefficiencies and limited reasoning capacity.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we propose an LLM programming workflow (LPW) designed to improve both initial code generation and subsequent refinements within a structured two-phase workflow.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, in the solution generation phase, the LLM first outlines a solution plan that decomposes the problem into manageable sub-problems and then verifies the generated solution plan through visible test cases.
elements.sent-2411-006: Subsequently, in the code implementation phase, the LLM initially drafts a code according to the solution plan and its verification.
elements.sent-2411-007: If the generated code fails the visible tests, the plan verification serves as the intended natural language solution to consistently inform the refinement process for correcting bugs.
elements.sent-2411-008: We further introduce SLPW, a sampling variant of LPW, which initially generates multiple solution plans and plan verifications, produces a program for each plan and its verification, and refines each program as necessary until one successfully passes the visible tests.
elements.sent-2411-009: Compared to the state-of-the-art methods across various existing LLMs, our experimental results show that LPW significantly improves the Pass@1 accuracy by up to 16.4\% on well-established text-to-code generation benchmarks, especially with a notable improvement of around 10\% on challenging benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Additionally, SLPW demonstrates up to a 5.6\% improvement over LPW and sets new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy on various benchmarks, e.g., 98.2\% on HumanEval, 84.8\% on MBPP, 64.0\% on APPS, and 35.3\% on CodeContest, using the advanced LLM GPT-4o as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-011: Code generation, also known as program synthesis, studies the automatic construction of a program that satisfies a specified high-level input requirement \citep{gulwani2017program .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Recently, large language models (LLMs) pre-trained on extensive code-related datasets \citep{brown2020language, meta2024introducing, li2023starcoder, roziere2023code, achiam2023gpt, muennighoff2023octopack have shown success in code-related tasks, such as code generation from natural language descriptions, also named as text-to-code generation~\citep{chen2021evaluating,austin2021program, li2022competition , code translation \citep{pan2024lost,yang2024exploring , and code completion \citep{izadi2024language .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, LLM-based code generation remains challenging due to stringent lexical, grammatical, and semantic constraints \citep{scholak2021picard .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To overcome these challenges, multiple initial programs are generated \citep{chen2021evaluating, chowdhery2023palm , followed by different best-program selection strategies to improve code generation performance over LLMs \citep{li2022competition, chen2022codet,zhang2023coder,ni2023lever .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Code generation substantially benefits from the empirical insights of human programmers.
elements.sent-2411-016: In practice, human programmers develop high-quality code by consistently identifying and rectifying errors through the analysis of test case executions, rather than a single effort \citep{huang2023survey, chen2023teaching .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Different studies have refined programs based on execution results and LLM-generated information such as code and error explanation \citep{tang2023explain,shinn2023reflexion,madaan2024self .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Recent work further optimizes refinement (debugging) methods by performing rubber duck debugging processes \citep{chen2023teaching and leveraging control flow graph information to assist LLMs in locating bugs \citep{zhong2024ldb .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Software development models such as Waterfall and Scrum underscore the importance of communication among various development roles in the production of high-quality software \citep{davis2012agile,schwaber2004agile,andrei2019study .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Motivated by this principle, several studies \citep{lin2024llm,qian2023communicative, dong2023self have employed LLM instances as customized agents, assigning them diverse development roles and facilitating their collaboration.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Multi-agent collaborative code generation emphasizes the distinct workload for each LLM agent, e.g., requirement analyst, architect, programmer, and tester \citep{lin2024llm .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Additionally, various communication strategies have been proposed to ensure program quality.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, \cite{hong2023metagpt introduced a communication protocol to ensure efficient interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, all the aforementioned methods have certain weaknesses.
elements.sent-2411-025: Multiple sampling approaches suffer from sampling inefficiency and conflict with human programming strategies.
elements.sent-2411-026: In code refinement, feedback messages often lack precise correction instructions, leading to numerous refinements that deviate from the intended solution.
elements.sent-2411-027: Additionally, refining programs that significantly diverge from the problem description remains an open challenge \citep{tian2023test .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In multi-agent collaborative code generation, ineffective feedback mechanisms degrade communication quality.
elements.sent-2411-029: This issue is exacerbated when an excessive number of agents are involved, resulting in increased token consumption \citep{huang2023agentcoder .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{code_generation_pipeline_color.pdf} \caption{The pipeline of LPW, a \textit{large language model programming workflow}, where the components highlighted in red are exclusive to LPW. LPW consists of two phases. In the solution generation phase, LPW initially creates a solution plan (block (b)) for a problem (block (a)), along with the plan verification (block (c)) for each visible test. If the plan verification infers the accurate output for each visible test based on the solution plan (block (c)) and no incorrect logic is found in the verification check process (block (d)), LPW uses the generated plan and plan verification to help LLMs draft the initial program (block (e)) at the beginning of the code implementation phase. If the initial program passes all visible tests after execution (block (f)), it is used as the final code (block (l)) and then assessed with hidden tests. Otherwise, the LLM-generated code explanation (block (g)) and error analysis (block (j)) serve as debugging inputs to refine the error program (block (k)). The LLM-generated error analysis involves comparing the execution trace (block (h)) with the plan verification (block (i)) on the failed visible test to identify logic flaws in the code implementation and provide repair suggestions. The refined program is reevaluated on the visible tests to determine the necessity for further debugging iterations.} \label{key_component}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we propose LPW, a large language model programming workflow , specifically for text-to-code generation, addressing the aforementioned limitations.
elements.sent-2411-031: LPW emulates Waterfall and Scrum where analysis and design steps are prioritized before code implementation.
elements.sent-2411-032: However, LPW streamlines traditional software development models into two phases: the solution generation phase for plan and plan verification creation, and the code implementation phase for initial code development and subsequent refinements.
elements.sent-2411-033: The pipeline of LPW is depicted in Figure \ref{key_component .}
elements.sent-2411-034: LPW leverages various information, including LLM-generated solution plan \citep{jiang2023self (block (b)), LLM-generated code explanation \citep{chen2023teaching (block (g)), and runtime information from program execution \citep{zhong2024ldb (block (h)) to boost the code generation performance, and efficiently incorporates them into an end-to-end framework.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: In LPW, aside from runtime information, all other messages are autonomously generated by LLMs using few-shot prompting, without the need for annotated corpora or additional training.
elements.sent-2411-036: A unique feature of LPW is the incorporation of the plan verification (block (c) in Figure~\ref{key_component ) as the natural language intended solution for visible tests to derive the reliable program solution.}
elements.sent-2411-037: LPW initially produces a solution plan that decomposes complex programs into several tractable sub-problems (intermediate steps) \citep{cheng2022binding, zelikman2023parsel, jiang2023self .}
elements.sent-2411-038: LPW then verifies the solution plan against visible tests to assess its correctness, known as plan verification.
elements.sent-2411-039: For a visible test, the verification includes a text-based, step-by-step analysis to derive the output for each intermediate step and the final output, ensuring that the final output is consistent with the visible test result.
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, each inferred intermediate output is reviewed by LLMs (block (d) in Figure~\ref{key_component ) to maintain logical consistency throughout the verification.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Different from other approaches that exclude the solution plan entirely from the code generation \citep{chen2023teaching, zhong2024ldb , LPW incorporates the LLM-generated plan and its verification in the initial code development to clarify the programming logic.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This approach ensures that the initial code closely aligns with the problem description, thus reducing the need for subsequent refinements.
elements.sent-2411-043: The plan verification encompasses comprehensive conditions and logical specifications for solving visible tests, eliminating potential misunderstandings before code generation.
elements.sent-2411-044: This is akin to Test-Driven Development, where human developers validate the intended solution with test cases \citep{beck2022test .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Furthermore, LPW consistently integrates plan verification in the subsequent refinements.
elements.sent-2411-046: In contrast to previous studies \citep{chen2023teaching, zhong2024ldb, shinn2023reflexion that query LLMs to infer errors in the generated code when it fails a visible test, LPW prompts LLMs to compare the expected output of each intermediate step for solving the failed visible test, as recorded in the plan verification, against the execution trace on the failed visible test to identify discrepancies and further produce an error analysis (block (j) in Figure \ref{key_component ).}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This approach is more straightforward and reduces uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-048: These discrepancies assist LLMs in accurately locating bugs and identifying logic flaws in the code implementation, and generating detailed refinement suggestions, as documented in the error analysis.
elements.sent-2411-049: Then, the error analysis when integrated with the code explanation serves as feedback to refine the code in LPW, surpassing conventional scalar or vector rewards and thereby improving the efficiency and accuracy of the refinement process.
elements.sent-2411-050: We further explore a sampling variant of LPW named as SLPW.
elements.sent-2411-051: SLPW leverages the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) algorithm \citep{auer2002finite to balance the exploration and exploitation in debugging multiple generated code samples for optimizing overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-052: We evaluate LPW and SLPW on four text-to-code generation benchmarks: HumanEval \citep{chen2021evaluating , MBPP \citep{austin2021program , and their extended test case variants, HumanEval-ET and MBPP-ET \citep{dong2023codescore .}}}

elements.sent-2411-055: The experiment results demonstrate that LPW and SLPW consistently improve text-to-code generation performance across all benchmarks and LLM backbones.
elements.sent-2411-056: Compared to the state-of-the-art LLM debugger, LDB \citep{zhong2024ldb , LPW improves Pass@1 accuracy by around 4\% across all benchmarks with the GPT-3.5 backbone and achieves up to 16.4\% improvement on MBPP when using Llama-3 as the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-057: SLPW shows an additional 1\% improvement over LPW with GPT-3.5 and increases accuracy by up to 5.6\% over LPW on MBPP with Phi-3.
elements.sent-2411-058: When tested with the advanced GPT-4o \citep{openAI2024GPT-4o backbone, LPW and SLPW maintain their advantages, and SLPW achieves new state-of-the-art performance across all benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Notably, on two challenging benchmarks, APPS \citep{hendrycks2021measuring and CodeContests \citep{li2022competition , LPW and SLPW improve Pass@1 accuracy by around 10\% and 5\%, respectively, compared to LDB with the GPT-4o backbone.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: We outline the key contributions in this paper as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-061: We introduce an end-to-end large language model programming workflow, LPW, that draws inspiration from conventional software development models while streamlining and tailoring them specifically for text-to-code generation.
elements.sent-2411-062: LPW significantly improves the code generation accuracy over the state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-063: In LPW, we derive the intended solution for visible tests, represented by the plan verification, through querying LLMs to validate the correctness of the LLM-generated solution plan on visible tests before code implementation.
elements.sent-2411-064: The plan verification clarifies all conditions, flow logic, arithmetic operations, and punctuation specifications required to solve the visible tests for the given problem, thereby increasing the LLMs' confidence during both the initial program generation and subsequent debugging processes.
elements.sent-2411-065: We investigate SLPW, a sampling variant of LPW, and show that debugging across multiple program samples can further enhance performance and set new state-of-the-art results.
elements.sent-2411-066: We conduct extensive experiments across six text-to-code generation benchmarks to validate the performance of LPW and SLPW with various LLM backbones, provide a comprehensive analysis of their performance and failure cases, and highlight the existing challenges.
elements.sent-2411-067: We follow the problem formulation for text-to-code generation as outlined in \cite{jiang2023self , \cite{chen2023teaching , and \cite{zhong2024ldb .}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The text-to-code generation problem is formulated as a triple $\mathcal{P =\langle Q, T_v, T_h \rangle$, where $Q$ represents the problem specifications described in natural language, and $T_v$ and $T_h$ are sets of visible and hidden tests, each containing input-output pairs $(t^i, t^o) \in T = T_v \cup T_h$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The goal is to leverage the LLM $\mathcal{M}$ to generate a program function $f$, $\mathcal{M \rightarrow f$, that maps each input $t^i$ to its corresponding output $t^o$ for all pairs in $T$, i.e., $f(t^i)=t^o$, for $(t^i, t^o) \in T$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: We note that $T_h$ remains hidden during both solution generation and code implementation phases and only becomes visible if the generated $f$ passes $T_v$.
elements.sent-2411-071: In LPW, for all components shown in Figure \ref{key_component , the problem description $Q$ is, by default, concatenated with task-specific prompts to produce the desired response from LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The detailed messages are available in Section \ref{case_study .. \end{wrapfigure.}}

elements.sent-2411-075: LPW leverages the self-planning approach introduced by \cite{jiang2023self to abstract and decompose the problem description $Q$ into a strategic and adaptable plan $\Pi$ at the start of the solution generation phase.}
elements.sent-2411-076: For a problem in HumanEval described by block (1) in Figure \ref{workflow_solution_generation , its example solution plan is illustrated at block (3).}
elements.sent-2411-077: However, the LLM-generated plan $\Pi$ may occasionally be incorrect, misguiding subsequent program generation.

elements.sent-2411-079: The LLM-responded plan verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ delivers a step-by-step analysis, including all intermediate results and final derived outputs for all visible tests $T_v$ based on $\Pi$.}

elements.sent-2411-097: These update methods ensure that the solution plan $\Pi$ and its verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ achieve the necessary precision, as well-formed $\Pi$ and $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ are essential for the subsequent code generation accuracy \citep{jiang2023self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: In the code implementation phase, the code refinement process acts as an update mechanism, replacing the program $f$ with the refined program $f^\prime$ when $f$ fails the visible test $T_v$ as highlighted by the red arrow in Figure \ref{workflow_code_implemtation .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Overall, for a problem $\mathcal{P}$, LPW iteratively revises the generated plan $\Pi$ and its verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$, in the solution generation phase, until $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ infers the correct outputs for all visible tests $T_v$ and no error intermediate outputs are present in $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Otherwise, LPW reports a failure for $\mathcal{P}$ when reaching the maximum iterations.
elements.sent-2411-101: Similarly, in the code implementation phase, LPW iteratively refines the generated program $f$ if bugs exist.
elements.sent-2411-102: This process continues until a refined $f$ successfully solves $T_v$, followed by Pass@1 accuracy calculation on hidden tests $T_h$, or LPW reports a failure for $\mathcal{P}$ upon reaching the maximum iteration limit.
elements.sent-2411-103: Text-to-code generation benefits from both multiple sampling and debugging.
elements.sent-2411-104: These two approaches have evolved orthogonally.
elements.sent-2411-105: We propose a sampling variant of LPW, referred as SLPW.
elements.sent-2411-106: SLPW follows the same workflow and update mechanism as LPW but incorporates multiple plan samples $\{\Pi_1,\ldots \Pi_k\}$ and program samples $\{f_1,\ldots f_q\}$.
elements.sent-2411-107: SLPW generates $k$ plan samples at the beginning of the solution generation phase.
elements.sent-2411-108: For each iteration, SLPW leverages the UCB algorithm to competitively select a plan $\Pi$ with the highest upper confidence interval.
elements.sent-2411-109: Then, SLPW performs the same verification process as LPW for $\Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-110: When SLPW verifies $\Pi$ over each visible test and the verification fails on a visible test, it uses the number of visible tests where the plan verification derives an accurate final output as a reward to update the confidence interval of $\Pi$, and $\Pi$ is replaced with the revised plan $\Pi^\prime$.
elements.sent-2411-111: Alternatively, when SLPW checks the correctness of intermediate outputs in the plan verification for each visible test and encounters erroneous values, it uses the number of visible tests where the plan verification contains correct intermediate outputs as a reward to update the confidence interval of $\Pi$.
elements.sent-2411-112: SLPW outputs the first $q$, where $q\leq k$, solution plans along with their verifications for the subsequent code implementation phase when solution plans and their verifications are confirmed as correct within the iteration threshold.
elements.sent-2411-113: Otherwise, SLPW provides $[0,q)$ solution plans and their verifications as the output after reaching the iteration threshold.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the code implementation phase, SLPW initially generates a program for each plan and its verification.
elements.sent-2411-115: If no initial program solves $T_v$, SLPW applies the UCB algorithm to optimize refinements across multiple programs.
elements.sent-2411-116: It selects a program $f$, refines it, and updates the confidence interval of $f$ based on the number of passed visible tests, until a refined program addresses $T_v$ and reports the Pass@1 accuracy on $T_h$.
elements.sent-2411-117: Otherwise, the process terminates with a failure upon reaching the iteration threshold.

elements.sent-2411-217: Program synthesis remains an open challenge of generating a program within a target domain-specific language (DSL) from given specifications.
elements.sent-2411-218: One prevalent approach involves searching the large space of possible programs.
elements.sent-2411-219: For example, generalized planning whose solution is formalized as a planning program with pointers \citep{segovia2024generalized, lei2023novelty has demonstrated promising results in synthesizing program solutions for abstract visual reasoning tasks \citep{lei2024generalized when the DSL is carefully designed.}}
elements.sent-2411-220: However, hand-crafted DSLs often suffer from limited generalization capacity, and the huge search space diminishes its effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-221: Recently, large language models trained on vast corpora have excelled in natural language processing (NLP) tasks and have been extended to code generation e.g., GPT-series \citep{achiam2023gpt, openAI2024GPT-4o , Llama-series \citep{meta2024introducing,roziere2023code,touvron2023llama , and Claude-series \citep{anthropic2024claude .}}}
elements.sent-2411-222: LPW and SLPW leverage the strengths of LLMs in NLP tasks to generate intended solutions in natural language.
elements.sent-2411-223: These text-based solutions demonstrate high-quality logical reasoning steps and satisfactory accuracy, thereby effectively aiding subsequent code generation.. Prompting Techniques.
elements.sent-2411-224: To imitate the logical chain in human brain when tackling reasoning tasks, prompting methods direct LLMs to decompose problems into solvable sub-problems \citep{jiang2023self,zhou2022least,lightman2023let,dhuliawala2023chain and progressively infer the correct answer with intermediate outputs, as exemplified by chain-of-thought prompting \citep{wei2022chain,kojima2022large .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Inspired by these studies, LPW and SLPW decompose a text-to-code problem into several sub-problems described by the solution plan and follow the chain-of-thought prompting idea to verify the solution plan against visible tests with step-by-step analysis.
elements.sent-2411-226: The generated plan and its verification provide step-by-step natural language instructions for code generation, aiding LLMs in both the initial code development and subsequent refinements.. Code Refinement.
elements.sent-2411-227: Accurate program solutions often require iterative refinements due to model limitations \citep{zhong2024ldb, chen2023teaching, shinn2023reflexion .}
elements.sent-2411-228: Various interactive approaches have been proposed to optimize debugging performance in LLMs, such as human feedback \cite{chen2023improving, le2022coderl, wu2024fine , trained models \citep{huang2023empirical, le2022coderl, yasunaga2021break , LLM-generated explanations \citep{chen2023teaching, madaan2024self, shinn2023reflexion, tang2023explain , and execution results \citep{zhong2024ldb, holtl2mac, tian2023test .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Current state-of-the-art LLM debuggers, such as Self-Debugging and LDB, repair various seed programs to create the program solution.
elements.sent-2411-230: However, they encounter difficulties when the initial code substantially deviates from the original intent.
elements.sent-2411-231: Besides, without safeguarding, the refined code frequently diverges from the problem specifications.
elements.sent-2411-232: In contrast, LPW and SLPW develop initial code that adheres to the validated intended solution through plan verification, minimizing deviations from the problem description.
elements.sent-2411-233: The plan verification further guides code refinement, ensuring alignment with the problem specifications.
elements.sent-2411-234: We propose LPW, a large language model programming workflow, for text-to-code generation tasks, which enables LLMs to accurately draft an initial program and effectively correct bugs.
elements.sent-2411-235: LPW uses various advanced code generation techniques and efficiently incorporates them into a two-phase development model.
elements.sent-2411-236: We further present SLPW, a sampling variant of LPW, where multiple initial programs are generated and then competitively refined as necessary.
elements.sent-2411-237: We evaluate LPW and SLPW on well-established text-to-code generation benchmarks across various LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-238: LPW significantly improves code generation accuracy compared to other existing approaches.
elements.sent-2411-239: SLPW achieves new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy, with 98.2\% on HumanEval, 84.8\% on MBPP, 64.0\% on APPS, and 35.3\% on CodeContests benchmarks using GPT-4o as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-240: These results highlight the effectiveness of our workflow in generating high-quality code and underscore the benefits of incorporating sampling and debugging.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{A problem description $Q$, large language mold backbone $\mathcal{M}$, visible tests $T_v$, maximum iterations for solution generation $I_s$, $k$ plan samples, $q$ output solution plans and their verifications. } \KwOut{ A set of plans with their verifications $S_{output}=\{(\Pi_1, \mathcal{A}(\Pi_1, T_v)),\ldots,(\Pi_q, \mathcal{A}(\Pi_q, T_v))\}$.} {\tcp{plan generation}} $plans \leftarrow \mathcal{M}(Q, k, t=0.4)$ { \tcp*[r]{generate $k$ initial $plans=\{\Pi_1,\ldots\Pi_{k}\}$ with temperature $t=0.4$}} $S_{output} \leftarrow \emptyset$\; $i_s \leftarrow 0$ { \tcp*[r]{set current iteration $i_s$ to 0}} $\mathrm{InitialUCB(len}(plans))$ { \tcp*[r]{initialize UCB algorithm with $\mathrm{len}(plans)$ arms}} \While{ $i_s<I_s$}{ $\Pi \leftarrow \mathrm{SelectArm} (plans)${ \tcp*[r]{ select an arm $\Pi$ in $plans$ leveraging UCB algorithm}} { \tcp{plan verification generation}} $\mathcal{A}(\Pi,T_v) \leftarrow \mathcal{M}(Q,\Pi,T_v)$\; $n, \Pi^\prime \leftarrow \mathcal{A}(\Pi,T_v)$ { \tcp*[r]{number of visible tests $n$ where the plan verification derives an accurate final output, revised plan $\Pi^\prime$ }} \If{$n= \mathrm{len}(T_v) $}{ { \tcp{verification intermediate output check}} $z \leftarrow \mathcal{M}(Q, \mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v),\Pi)$ { \tcp*[r]{number of visible tests $z$ where the plan verification contains correct intermediate outputs}} \If{$z= \mathrm{len}(T_v)$} { $S_{output} \leftarrow S_{output} \cup (\Pi, \mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v))$ { \tcp*[r]{ save $(\Pi$, $\mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v))$ if no error in $\mathcal{A}$}} Delete($plans, \Pi$) { \tcp*[r]{delete arm $\Pi$ from $plans$ in UCB}} \If{$\mathrm{len}(S_{output})=q$} { break { \tcp*[r]{return first $q$ solution plans and their verifications}} } } \Else{ UpdateConfidence($\Pi, z$) { \tcp*[r]{ update the confidence interval of $\Pi$ with reward $z$ }} } } \Else { $plans(\Pi) \leftarrow \Pi^\prime$ { \tcp*[r]{ replace $\Pi$ in $plans$ with revised plan $\Pi^\prime$ }} UpdateConfidence($\Pi, n$) { \tcp*[r]{ update the confidence interval of $\Pi$ with reward $n$}} } $i_s \leftarrow i_s+1$\; } \If{$\mathrm{len}(S_{output})=0$}{\Return{$\{\}$} \tcp*[r]{return empty set if neither a valid plan nor its verification is created}} \Return{$S_{output}$}\; \caption{SLPW: Solution Generation Phase} \label{alg:solution_gen}

for code implementation $I_c$. } \KwOut{Final code $f$.} $programs \leftarrow \emptyset$\; $i_c \leftarrow 0$ { \tcp*[r]{set current iteration $i_c$ to 0}} {\tcp{initial code generation}} \For{$\mathrm{plan}$ $\Pi$, $\mathrm{verification}$ $\mathcal{A}(\Pi,T_v)$ $\mathrm{in}$ $S_{output}$} {$f\leftarrow \mathcal{M}(Q, \Pi, \mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v))${ \tcp*[r]{generate an initial program $f$ for each $\Pi$ and $\mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v)$}} $solved \leftarrow \mathrm{Exe}(f,T_v)$ { \tcp*[r]{return a boolean result $solved$ after executing $f$ on $T_v$}} \If{$solved$}{ \Return{$f$} { \tcp*[r]{return $f$ when $f$ passes $T_v$ }}} $programs \leftarrow programs \cup (f, \mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v))$ { \tcp*[r]{record ($f$, $\mathcal{A}$) for further refinements}} } $\mathrm{InitialUCB(len}(programs))$ { \tcp*[r]{initialize UCB algorithm with len($programs$) arms}} \While{ $i_c<I_c$}{ $f, \mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v) \leftarrow \mathrm{SelectArm} (programs)${ \tcp*[r]{ select an arm ($f$, $\mathcal{A}$) using UCB algorithm}} $f_{p} \leftarrow \mathcal{M}(Q, f, \mathcal{A}(\Pi, T_v))$ { \tcp*[r]{add print statements in $f$ resulting in $f_p$}} $n, \Bar{t_{v}}, \mathcal{T}(f,\Bar{t_{v}}), \leftarrow \mathrm{Exe}(f_{p},T_v)$ { \tcp*[r]{the number of solved visible tests $n$, execution trace $\mathcal{T}$ on the first failed visible test $\Bar{t_{v}}$ after elements.sent-2411-304: Conversely, if the plan contains logical errors or omits edge cases, the verification process replicates these mistakes.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Refinement Consistency in LPW and SLPW
elements.sent-2411-319: LPW and SLPW allow multiple rounds of debugging to refine code based on error analysis generated by comparing the code execution trace and plan verification on the failed visible test.
elements.sent-2411-320: Additionally, LPW and SLPW query LLMs to generate refined code accompanied by a refinement explanation, detailing the modifications implemented to address the errors identified in the error analysis.
elements.sent-2411-321: For instance, Figures \ref{debugging_analysis and \ref{table:case_study illustrate two HumanEval problems where LPW successfully generates the correct program through refinements informed by the error analysis using the GPT-3.5 backbone.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: We note that in LPW and SLPW, if the refined code is irrelevant to the error analysis, the entire debugging process degrades to a simple sampling approach, contradicting our original intent.
elements.sent-2411-323: As a result, we manually evaluate the debugging consistency among the generated error analysis (part (e)), the refined code (part (f)), and the refinement explanation (part (g)), as exampled in Figure \ref{debugging_analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-324: In LPW, only one refined code deviates from the error analysis yet still produces the correct solution, across all problems solved through debugging.
elements.sent-2411-325: SLPW achieves perfect consistency between the error analysis and the refined code.
elements.sent-2411-326: These results validate the effectiveness of the debugging steps in the code implementation phase for both LPW and SLPW, where the meaningful error analysis enables LLMs to produce the correct program with precise refinements.

elements.sent-2411-363: The strong performance of large language models (LLMs) raises extensive discussion on their application to code generation.
elements.sent-2411-364: Recent research suggests continuous program refinements through visible tests to improve code generation accuracy in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-365: However, these methods suffer from LLMs' inefficiency and limited reasoning capacity.
elements.sent-2411-366: Specifically, the solution generation phase formulates a solution plan, which is then verified through visible tests to specify the intended natural language solution.
elements.sent-2411-367: Subsequently, the code implementation phase drafts an initial code according to the solution plan and its verification.
elements.sent-2411-368: If the generated code fails the visible tests, the plan verification serves as the intended solution to consistently inform the refinement process for correcting bugs.
elements.sent-2411-369: Compared to state-of-the-art methods across various existing LLMs, LPW significantly improves the Pass@1 accuracy by up to 16.4\% on well-established text-to-code generation benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-370: LPW also sets new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy, achieving 98.2\% on HumanEval, 84.8\% on MBPP, 59.3\% on LiveCode, 62.6\% on APPS, and 34.7\% on CodeContest, using GPT-4o as the backbone.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{code_generation_pipeline_color.pdf} \caption{The pipeline of LPW, where the components highlighted in red are exclusive to LPW.} \label{key_component}
elements.sent-2411-371: In practice, human programmers develop high-quality code by consistently rectifying errors through the analysis of test case executions \citep{huang2023survey, chen2023teaching .}
elements.sent-2411-372: Recent work further optimizes refinement (debugging) by performing rubber duck debugging processes \citep{chen2023teaching and leveraging control flow graph information to assist LLMs in locating bugs \citep{zhong2024ldb .}}
elements.sent-2411-373: However, the absence of precise correction instructions in feedback messages results in numerous refinements that deviate from the intended solution.
elements.sent-2411-374: To replicate each phase of program development, several studies \citep{lin2024llm,qian2023communicative, dong2023self have employed LLM instances as customized agents, assigning them diverse roles and facilitating their collaboration.}
elements.sent-2411-375: Recent work incorporates extra visible tests \citep{huang2023agentcoder and solution plans \citep{islam-etal-2024-mapcoder generated by designated agents to improve the code refinements in multi-agent collaborations.}}
elements.sent-2411-376: However, the absence of methodologies for generating reliable visible tests and plans in these studies undermines their credibility, as incorrect visible tests and plans can lead to erroneous codes.
elements.sent-2411-377: Besides, with an increased number of agents, multi-agent collaborations consume significant token resources for communication \citep{huang2023agentcoder .}
elements.sent-2411-378: The detailed related work is discussed in Appendix \ref{Related_Work .}
elements.sent-2411-379: LPW involves two phases for code generation: the solution generation phase for plan creation and plan verification, and the code implementation phase for initial code development and subsequent refinements.
elements.sent-2411-380: LPW leverages various information, including LLM-generated solution plan \citep{jiang2023self (block (b)), LLM-generated code explanation \citep{chen2023teaching (block (g)), and runtime information from program execution \citep{zhong2024ldb (block (h)) to boost the code generation performance, and effectively incorporates them into an end-to-end framework.}}}
elements.sent-2411-381: A unique feature of LPW is incorporating plan verification (block (c)) as the natural language intended solution for visible tests.
elements.sent-2411-382: LPW initially produces a solution plan that decomposes a complex problem into several tractable sub-problems (intermediate steps) (block (b)).
elements.sent-2411-383: For a visible test, the LLM-generated verification includes a text-based step-by-step analysis to derive the output for each intermediate step and the final output, ensuring that the final output is consistent with the visible test result.
elements.sent-2411-384: Subsequently, each inferred intermediate output is reviewed by LLMs (block (d)) to maintain logical consistency and mitigate hallucination throughout the verification.
elements.sent-2411-385: LPW uses the plan and its verification in the initial code development (block (e)) to ensure that the initial code closely aligns with the problem description.
elements.sent-2411-386: Furthermore, LPW incorporates plan verification in the subsequent refinements.
elements.sent-2411-387: By comparing discrepancies between the expected output of each intermediate step, as recorded in the plan verification, against the execution trace on the failed visible test (block (h)), LPW accurately locates bugs, identifies logic flaws in the code implementation, and further generates detailed refinement suggestions, as documented in the error analysis (block (j)).
elements.sent-2411-388: Then, the error analysis when integrated with the code explanation (block (g)) serves as feedback to refine the code, surpassing conventional scalar rewards or deduced error analysis \citep{chen2023teaching, zhong2024ldb, shinn2023reflexion and thereby improving refinement efficiency and accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-389: We first evaluate LPW on four text-to-code generation benchmarks: HumanEval \citep{chen2021evaluating , MBPP \citep{austin2021program , and their extended test case variants, HumanEval-ET and MBPP-ET \citep{dong2023codescore .}}}
elements.sent-2411-390: Compared to the state-of-the-art LLM debugger, LDB \citep{zhong2024ldb , LPW improves Pass@1 accuracy by 2\% to 6.1\% across all benchmarks with GPT-3.5 and achieves up to 16.4\% improvement on MBPP with Llama-3.}
elements.sent-2411-391: When evaluated on additional benchmarks using the advanced GPT-4o \citep{openAI2024GPT-4o , LPW maintains its advantages, and achieves new state-of-the-art performance across evaluated benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-392: Notably, on the contamination-free benchmark, LiveCode \citep{jain2024livecodebench , and challenging benchmarks, APPS \citep{hendrycks2021measuring and CodeContests \citep{li2022competition , LPW improves Pass@1 accuracy by around 5\%, 10\%, and 5\%, respectively, compared to LDB.}}}
elements.sent-2411-393: We outline our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-394: We introduce an end-to-end large language model programming workflow, LPW, which significantly improves the code generation accuracy over the state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-395: We derive the intended solution for visible tests, represented as the plan verification.
elements.sent-2411-396: The plan verification clarifies logic specifications required to solve the visible tests for the given problem, thereby increasing the LLMs' confidence during both the initial program generation and subsequent debugging processes.
elements.sent-2411-397: We conduct extensive experiments across seven text-to-code generation benchmarks to validate the performance of LPW with various LLM backbones, provide a comprehensive analysis of their performance, token usage, and failure cases, and highlight the existing challenges.
elements.sent-2411-398: We follow the problem formulation for text-to-code generation as outlined in \citet{jiang2023self , \citet{chen2023teaching , and \citet{zhong2024ldb .}}}
elements.sent-2411-399: The text-to-code generation problem is formulated as a triple $\mathcal{P =\langle Q, T_v, T_h \rangle$, where $Q$ represents the natural language problem specifications, and $T_v$ and $T_h$ are sets of visible and hidden tests, each containing input-output pairs $(t^i, t^o) \in T = T_v \cup T_h$.}
elements.sent-2411-400: The goal is to leverage the LLM $\mathcal{M}$ to generate a program function $f$, $\mathcal{M \rightarrow f$, that maps each input $t^i$ to its output $t^o$ for all pairs in $T$, i.e., $f(t^i)=t^o$, for $(t^i, t^o) \in T$.}
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{solution_generation_work_flow_edit.pdf} \caption{ (a): An illustrated workflow of the solution generation phase in LPW. (b): Example message fragments corresponding to each workflow component for a HumanEval problem (120th) with the GPT-3.5 backbone. The detailed messages are available in Section \ref{case_study}.} \label{workflow_solution_generation}
elements.sent-2411-401: LPW leverages the self-planning approach introduced by \citet{jiang2023self to abstract and decompose the problem description $Q$ into a strategic and adaptable plan $\Pi$ at the start of the solution generation phase.}
elements.sent-2411-402: For each $t_v \in T_v$, its verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, \{t_v\})$ compares the derived output $t_v^{o^\prime}$ with the ground-truth output $t_v^o$ to assess the correctness of $\Pi$, as outlined at block 4.}
elements.sent-2411-403: If all intermediate outputs in $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ are validated as correct by the LLM as shown at block 5, $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ is treated as the intended solution for $T_v$.}}
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{code_implementation_work_flow_edit.pdf} \caption{(a): An illustrated workflow of the code implementation phase in LPW. (b): Example message fragments extending from Figure \ref{workflow_solution_generation} and corresponding to each workflow component. See Section \ref{case_study} for detailes. } \label{workflow_code_implemtation}
elements.sent-2411-404: LPW develops an initial program $f$ by prompting the LLM with the problem description $Q$ (block (1) in Figure \ref{workflow_solution_generation ), along with plan $\Pi$ and its verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ from the solution generation phase.}}
elements.sent-2411-405: LPW instructs the LLM to conduct an error analysis by identifying inconsistencies between the intermediate outputs recorded in the execution trace of $\Bar{t_v}$ and the expected intermediate outputs documented in the verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi,\{\Bar{t_v}\})$, analyzing causes, and offering refinement suggestions (block (4)).}
elements.sent-2411-406: Subsequently, the error analysis and code explanation for $f$ generated by the LLM (block (5)) are concatenated as the prompt to generate the refined program $f^\prime$ (block (6)).
elements.sent-2411-407: LPW replaces $f$ with the refined program $f^\prime$ and revalidates the updated $f$ against the visible tests $T_v$ to assess the need for further refinements.. Iterative Updates.
elements.sent-2411-408: LPW includes two update steps in the solution generation phase to enable self-correction as indicated by the red arrows in Figure \ref{workflow_solution_generation : 1) when the plan verification inferred final output differs from the ground-truth output for a visible test, where $t_v^{o^\prime \neq t_v^o, \exists t_v \in T_v$ in $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$, a revised solution plan $\Pi^\prime$ is included in the LLM response to substitute the original plan; 2) when the LLM detects any incorrect intermediate values in $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ e.g., contextual inconsistencies, mathematical miscalculations, or logical flaws, LPW prompts the LLM to regenerate the plan verification.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-409: These update methods ensure that the solution plan $\Pi$ and its verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ maintain the necessary precision, as well-formed $\Pi$ and $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ are essential for accurate code generation \citep{jiang2023self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-410: Overall, for a problem $\mathcal{P}$, LPW iteratively revises the generated plan $\Pi$ and its verification $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$, in the solution generation phase, until $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$ infers correct outputs for all visible tests $T_v$ and no error intermediate outputs are present in $\mathcal{A (\Pi, T_v)$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-411: We first evaluate LPW on the well-established text-to-code benchmarks HumanEval, MBPP, HumanEval-ET, and MBPP-ET, where the given context outlines the intended functionality of the program to be synthesized.
elements.sent-2411-412: HumanEval-ET and MBPP-ET introduce approximately 100 additional hidden tests, covering numerous edge cases, for each problem in HumanEval and MBPP, thus being regarded as more reliable benchmarks ~\citep{dong2023codescore .}
elements.sent-2411-413: We compare LPW with the representative code generation approaches Self-Planning (SP)~\citep{jiang2023self , MapCoder ~\citep{islam-etal-2024-mapcoder , Self-Debugging (+Expl) (SD) ~\citep{chen2023teaching , and Large Language Model Debugger (LDB)~\citep{zhong2024ldb .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-414: SP relies solely on the LLM-generated solution plan to produce the program solution in a single effort without refinements.
elements.sent-2411-415: MapCoder, a multi-agent collaborative approach, generates multiple unverified plans and traverses them to produce and refine code based on the current plan.
elements.sent-2411-416: LDB, a state-of-the-art LLM debugger, segments generated programs into blocks based on the control flow graph, which facilitates bug detection and the refinement of each program block using runtime information in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-417: A detailed comparison of different methods is summarized in Appendix Table \ref{features_stratgies .}
elements.sent-2411-418: We generate a seed program for each problem with the same prompts and parameters introduced by \citet{chen2023teaching for SD and LDB and label the performance of seed programs as Baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-419: We note that SD and LDB only perform refinements on the seed program that fails the visible tests.
elements.sent-2411-420: We experiment with various LLMs with different parameter sizes, including GPT-3.5 (turbo-0125, $\geq$175B), Llama-3 (70B-Instruct), and Phi-3 (14B-Instruct) to evaluate performance and demonstrate that LPW is model-independent.
elements.sent-2411-422: We apply 2-shot prompting in LPW, with a maximum of 12 iterations for both the solution generation and code implementation phases.
elements.sent-2411-423: Similarly, we set the maximum debugging iterations to 12 for SD and LDB.
elements.sent-2411-424: MapCoder generates 3 plans, each with up to 4 refinement iterations.
elements.sent-2411-425: All following experiments adhere to these iteration settings.
elements.sent-2411-426: An empirical discussion on the number of iterations for LPW is available in Appendix \ref{Parameter_study .}
elements.sent-2411-428: Table \ref{table:detail_information presents the Pass@1 accuracy for evaluated approaches, along with their respective improvements over Baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-429: LPW outperforms all competing methods across all benchmarks and with various LLM backbones.
elements.sent-2411-430: Compared to LDB, LPW improves Pass@1 accuracy by 6.1\%, 4.8\% 3.6\%, and 2\%, on HumanEval, HumanEval-ET, MBPP, and MBPP-ET, respectively, with GPT-3.5 and achieves up to 16.4\% improvement on MBPP with Llama-3.
elements.sent-2411-431: These results showcase the effectiveness of the proposed workflow and demonstrate the model-independent benefits of LPW.

elements.sent-2411-483: We introduce LPW, a large language model programming workflow, designed for text-to-code generation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-484: LPW effectively integrates various advanced code generation techniques within a two-phase development model.
elements.sent-2411-485: A key innovation of LPW is the incorporation of plan verification, which enables LLMs to accurately draft an initial program and effectively correct bugs.
elements.sent-2411-486: We evaluate LPW on well-established text-to-code generation benchmarks across various LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-487: LPW significantly improves code generation accuracy compared to other existing approaches and achieves new state-of-the-art Pass@1 accuracy, with 98.2\% on HumanEval, 84.8\% on MBPP, 59.3\% on LiveCode, 62.6\% on APPS, and 34.7\% on CodeContests benchmarks using GPT-4o as the backbone.

elements.sent-2411-492: In the future, reducing this consumption remains a critical area for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-493: Besides, although the LLM-generated plan and plan verification demonstrate promising accuracy on current tasks, the accuracy of the generated code still lags behind that of the plan and plan verification (Appendix \ref{output_gpt-3.5 ).}
elements.sent-2411-494: Incorporating alternative solution representations \citep{zelikman2023parsel alongside natural language representations may assist LLMs in translating text-based solutions into program solutions more accurately.. .}

elements.sent-2411-496: These text-based solutions demonstrate high-quality logical reasoning steps and satisfactory accuracy, thereby effectively aiding subsequent code generation.
elements.sent-2411-497: Inspired by these studies, LPW decomposes a text-to-code problem into several sub-problems described by the solution plan and follows the chain-of-thought prompting idea to verify the solution plan against visible tests with step-by-step analysis.

elements.lstlisting-2411-002: [ caption=Prompt for plan verification, label=prm:plan_verification, ] <@======================================= System =======================================@> You are a logical reasoner tasked with performing a step-by-step analysis to derive the correct solution to the given problem based on the provided plan. <@======================================== User ========================================@> You will be presented with several plan verification examples, each starting with [Start Example] and ending with [End Example]. In each example, you will be given a Python writing problem, starting with [Example Problem Description], followed by the solution plan starting with [Example Solution Plan], and its verification process beginning with [Example Plan Verification for X] for a test case X, starting with [Example Test Cases]. During the verification process, intermediate variables that need to be recorded are clearly identified at the outset, starting with [Record Analysis]. Whenever the value of a recorded intermediate variable is updated, the new result is clearly displayed, beginning with [Record]. After the verification, the derived result is compared to the correct test result, starting with [Results Compare]. If the derived result matches the test result, the output will be [Correct Plan]. If the derived result differs from the test result, the output will be [Incorrect Plan], followed by the reasons for the discrepancy, starting with [Incorrect Reasons], and the revised correct solution plan, beginning with [Start Revised Solution Plan] and ending with [End Revised Solution Plan]. [Start Example] [Example Problem Description] def prime_number(n: int): """ In range 0 to 100, returns n-th number that is a prime. """ [Example Solution Plan] 1. Iterate number through 0 to 100. 2. Check each number, if it's prime. 3. Keep track of the count of prime numbers found. 4. Stop when we find the n-th prime number. 5. Return the nth prime number. [Example Test Cases] assert prime_number(3)==5 [Example Plan Verification for assert prime_number(2)==3] [Record Analysis] The return value is the nth prime number, so all nth prime numbers need to be clearly recorded! 1. Call the function prime_number(2). 2. According to line 1 in solution plan, Iterate number through 0 to 100. 3. According to line 2 in solution plan, Check if 0 is prime. It's not. 4. Move to next number 1. 5. According to line 2 in solution plan, Check if 1 is prime. It's not. 6. Move to next number 2. 7. According to line 2 in solution plan, Check if 2 is prime. It is a prime. 8. According to line 3 in solution plan, the count of prime numbers is 1. [Record]: 1st prime number is 2 9. Move to next number 3. 10. According to line 2 in solution plan, Check if 3 is prime. It is a prime. 11. According to line 3 in solution plan, the count of prime numbers is 2. [Record]: 2nd prime number is 3 12. According to line 4 in solution plan, Stop when we find the 2nd prime number. 13. According to line 5 in solution plan, Return the 2nd prime number, which is 3. [Results Compare] The test correct output is 3. The logic analysis output is 3. 3=3. Thus, the plan is verified to correctly handle all test cases. [Correct Plan] [End Example] ... Authors' notes: We omit another example for conciseness. The full prompt can be found in our released code. ... Finally, you will be given a problem description, beginning with [Problem Description], along with your generated solution plan, starting with [Solution Plan], to solve the [Problem Description], and multiple test cases starting with [Test Cases]. The phrase "Let's verify the plan" will indicate the beginning of the verification process, followed by your verification steps to confirm whether your generated plan can pass all test cases. For each test case, the verification must include [Record Analysis] to track the intermediate variables at the beginning. If any intermediate variable value is updated during the reasoning process, the updated value should be clearly displayed, starting with [Record]. Please include [Results Compare] to assess the derived outcome against the correct test output. If the derived result matches the test result, output [Correct Plan] and proceed to the next test case. If the derived result does not match the test result, output [Incorrect Plan], followed by the reasons for the discrepancy, starting with [Incorrect Reasons]. Finally, provide the revised solution plan, starting with [Start Revised Solution Plan] and ending with [End Revised Solution Plan], to complete the process.

elements.lstlisting-2411-004: [ caption=Prompt for initial code generation, label=prm:initial_code, ] <@======================================= System =======================================@> You are a Python writing assistant that only responds with Python programs to solve a Python writing problem. <@======================================== User ========================================@> You will receive several examples, each structured as follows, beginning with [Start Example] and ending with [End Example]. Finally, you will be provided with a Python writing problem, starting with [Problem Description]. A solution plan will follow, beginning with [Solution Plan].
